context: and we find that he says this in chapter number three and verse number 12, and now it is true that I am thy near kinsman, albeit there is a kinsman nearer than I.
point_summary: Boaz's acknowledgment of a nearer kinsman in Ruth 3:12 typologically identifies the law as the closer redeemer who ultimately cannot redeem, pointing forward to Christ as the true kinsman-redeemer.

context: but we find that as we look at the truth of the book of Ruth and we find that as Jesus is our kinsman redeemer in that picture that is given here in the book of Ruth
point_summary: Boaz's insistence on following the prescribed legal process before redeeming Ruth typifies Christ, who fulfilled the law completely, while the nearer kinsman who refused represents the law itself, which condemns but cannot redeem.

context: Notice in verse number 11 of Ruth chapter 3 and I'm jumping, I'll read verse 10, we'll read the context. In verse 10 he said to Ruth, blessed be thou the Lord my daughter for thou hast showed more kindness in the latter end than at the beginning and as much as thou followest not young men whether poor or rich
point_summary: Of the three times the phrase "virtuous woman" appears in Scripture, only once does God directly apply it to a named individual, making Ruth's designation uniquely significant.

context: Boaz says to her in verse number 11 of our text, I will do to thee all that thou requirest.
point_summary: Boaz's declaration in Ruth 3:11 illustrates that a woman's recognized virtue moves others to act sacrificially on her behalf, making her, as Proverbs 31 says, worth far more than rubies.

context: She heard that God had visited his people according to verse number six and gave them bread.
point_summary: Ruth 1:6 is cited to establish context: Naomi returns to her homeland because she heard — not yet experienced — that God had visited His people and provided bread amid famine.

context: The Bible says that she says, notice if you will, in verse number 11, and Naomi said, turn again, my daughters. Why will you go with me?
point_summary: Naomi's plea for her daughters-in-law to return home sets the stage for Ruth's defining act of loyalty, which the sermon uses to establish that a virtuous woman is identifiable by her choices.

context: Turn again, my daughters, go your way for I am too old to have a husband. If I should say I have hope, if I should have a husband also tonight and should also bear sons, would you tarry for them till they were grown? Would you stay for them from having husbands? Nay, my daughters, for it grieveth me much for your sakes that the hand of the Lord has gone out against me.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: They lifted up their voice and wept again and Orpah kissed her mother-in-law, but notice this, the Bible says, Ruth, clave unto her.
point_summary: Ruth's clinging to Naomi at the moment of Orpah's departure marks the decisive contrast between the two daughters-in-law, establishing Ruth's loyalty as the defining characteristic the sermon traces forward.

context: In verse number 15 of chapter one, the Bible says that she said, behold, thy sister-in-law has gone back unto her people and unto her gods. Return thou after thy sister-in-law.
point_summary: Naomi's urging Ruth to return to her people and their false gods sets the stage for Ruth's decisive refusal, revealing her extraordinary loyalty and commitment to Naomi's God.

context: Ruth said, entreat me not to leave thee, in verse number 16, or to return from following after thee. For whither thou goest, I will go, and where thou lodgest, I will lodge. Thy people shall be my people, and thy God, my God.
point_summary: Ruth's declaration in 1:16 is presented as the defining moment of her steadfast loyalty to Naomi, choosing Naomi's people and God over returning to her own people and their false gods as Orpah had done.

context: Where thou diest, will I die, and there will I be buried. The Lord do so to me, and more also, if ought, but death part thee and me.
point_summary: Ruth's oath in verse 17 — "where thou diest, will I die" — is cited as evidence of her steadfast, covenant-level commitment to Naomi, distinguishing her loyalty from Orpah's departure back to her people and false gods.

context: When she saw that she was steadfastly minded to go with her, then she left speaking unto her.
point_summary: Ruth's steadfast resolve — seen in her clinging to Naomi even after Naomi urged her to return to her people and their false gods — marks her as a woman of unwavering loyalty and genuine faith.

context: The Bible tells us in verse 19, so they too went until they came to Bethlehem, and it came to pass, notice what it says, when they were come to Bethlehem, that all the city was moved about them, and they said, is this Naomi?
point_summary: Ruth's arrival in Bethlehem with Naomi stirred the whole city, marking the moment her loyal commitment to follow Naomi—and Naomi's God—became publicly visible.

context: She said unto them, call me not Naomi, call me Mara, for the Almighty hath dealt very bitterly with me.
point_summary: Naomi's bitter self-renaming to "Mara" upon returning to Bethlehem frames her arrival as one marked by divine affliction and personal emptiness, setting the backdrop against which Ruth's loyalty is displayed.

context: I went out full, and the Lord hath brought me home again empty. Why then call you me Naomi, seeing the Lord hath testified against me,
point_summary: Naomi's self-description as one who "went out full" but was "brought home again empty" frames her bitter providence as the backdrop against which Ruth's loyal faithfulness stands in sharp contrast.

context: Boaz says, for all the city of my people doth know that thou art a virtuous woman.
point_summary: Boaz's declaration that "all the city" knew Ruth as virtuous demonstrates that a godly character, built over mere weeks of observable obedience, produces an undeniable public testimony.

context: We read it at the end of chapter number one, is not this Naomi?
point_summary: Ruth 1:19's account of Naomi's return to Bethlehem establishes the public context in which Ruth's character would be observed and evaluated by the whole city over the following weeks.

context: Boaz says, the whole city of my people doth know that thou art a virtuous woman.
point_summary: Boaz's public recognition of Ruth as a virtuous woman illustrates that a godly testimony is observable by others, calling believers to guard their conduct so Christ—not themselves—is seen as the source of any good in their lives.

context: And it says in verse number nine, and Boaz said unto the elders and unto all the people, ye are witnesses this day that I have bought all that was Alimelech's and all that was Chilion's and Malon's of the hand of Naomi.
point_summary: Boaz's public declaration before witnesses in Ruth 4:9 illustrates that Ruth's patient waiting on God and trusting His prescribed process ultimately resulted in her redemption and marriage — a fruit of her virtuous influence.

context: So Boaz took Ruth, and she was his wife, and when he went in unto her, the Lord gave her conception, and she bare a son.
point_summary: Ruth's marriage to Boaz and the Lord's gift of conception illustrate that virtuous character—demonstrated through singleness and waiting—carries forward into every new phase of life God opens.

context: Naomi took the child and laid it in her bosom and became nurse unto it.
point_summary: Naomi's role nursing Obed illustrates the generational, grandmotherly influence that virtuous women carry, an influence the sermon traces forward through Obed to Jesse to David.

context: In verse 13 of Ruth chapter number four, the Bible says this. So Boaz took Ruth.
point_summary: Rahab's faith produced a historical reward traceable through Ruth 4:13, placing her in the messianic lineage that runs from Boaz and Ruth through David to Jesus Christ.

context: the Lord gave her conception and she bare a son
point_summary: Ruth's faithful union with Boaz, the kinsman-redeemer, produced Obed, linking her directly to the Davidic line and ultimately to Jesus Christ as a historical reward for her faith.

context: blessed be the Lord which hath not left thee this day without a kinsman
point_summary: Ruth's faithfulness as a Gentile woman is shown to carry lasting historical reward, tracing a direct genealogical line through Obed and Jesse to David and ultimately to Jesus Christ.

context: thy daughter-in-law, which loveth thee, which is better to thee than seven sons, hath born him
point_summary: Ruth's faithful love for Naomi is shown to carry generational consequence, placing her directly in the ancestral line that runs from Boaz through Obed, Jesse, and David to Jesus Christ.

context: Naomi took the child and laid it in her bosom and became nurse unto it
point_summary: Ruth's act of faith produced a lineage—through Obed, Jesse, and David—that culminated historically in Jesus Christ, illustrating the far-reaching generational reward of trusting God.

context: they called his name Obed
point_summary: Ruth's faithful obedience is shown to carry generational consequence, placing her directly in the ancestral line that runs from Boaz through Obed, Jesse, and David to Jesus Christ.

context: Pharaohs begat Hezron, Hezron begat Ram, and Ram begat Amminadab, and Amminadab begat Nashon, and Nashon begat Salmon, Salmon begat Boaz, and Boaz begat Obed, and Obed begat Jesse, and Jesse begat David
point_summary: Ruth 4:18's genealogy is read as historical evidence that Rahab's faith produced a lineage culminating in David and ultimately Jesus Christ.

context: cleaving unto the Lord from the book of Ruth
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.
